title: Apollo 11 lunar landing
summary: On 20 July 1969 the American lunar module Eagle set down on the Sea of Tranquillity and, some six and a half hours later, Neil Armstrong stepped onto the surface of another world. Buzz Aldrin followed him; Michael Collins circled overhead in the command module. Apollo 11 fulfilled a promise made by John F. Kennedy in 1961, closed the most dramatic chapter of the Cold War Space Race, and remains the only occasion on which human beings have set foot on the Moon so far.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The mission grew out of a superpower contest. After the Soviet Union placed the first satellite, the first man, and the first woman in orbit, President John F. Kennedy told a joint session of Congress in May 1961 that the United States should commit itself, before the decade was out, to landing a man on the Moon and returning him safely. NASA's Apollo programme spent roughly 25 billion dollars of 1960s money and at its peak employed some 400,000 people across industry, universities, and government.

The hardware, the giant Saturn V rocket, the command and service module, and the two-stage lunar module, was proved in the Apollo 7 through Apollo 10 flights of 1968 and early 1969. The crew of Apollo 11 was announced in January 1969: Neil Armstrong, a former naval aviator and X-15 test pilot who had commanded Gemini 8, as mission commander; Air Force colonel Edwin 'Buzz' Aldrin, an MIT-trained orbital rendezvous specialist and Gemini 12 spacewalker, as lunar module pilot; and Michael Collins, veteran of Gemini 10, as command module pilot.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
Apollo 11 lifted off from Launch Complex 39A at the Kennedy Space Center in Florida on 16 July 1969 at 13:32 UTC, watched by an estimated one million people along the Florida coast and hundreds of millions on television. After a translunar coast of some seventy-six hours the craft entered lunar orbit on 19 July.

On 20 July Armstrong and Aldrin transferred to the lunar module Eagle, undocked, and began their descent while Collins remained aboard the command module Columbia. The landing was not routine: an overloaded guidance computer flashed alarm codes, and Armstrong took manual control to steer past a boulder-strewn crater. Eagle touched down at 20:17 UTC in a flat basaltic plain in the Mare Tranquillitatis, with, by later estimates, less than thirty seconds of descent fuel remaining. Armstrong radioed, 'Houston, Tranquility Base here, the Eagle has landed.'

At 02:56 UTC on 21 July, some six and a half hours after touchdown, Armstrong climbed down the ladder and set his left boot in the lunar dust, saying, 'That's one small step for [a] man, one giant leap for mankind.' Aldrin joined him nineteen minutes later. Together they spent about two hours and thirty-one minutes outside, planting an American flag and a plaque, deploying instruments including a seismometer and a laser retroreflector, and collecting about 21.5 kilograms of lunar rock and soil. President Richard Nixon spoke to them by telephone from the White House.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Eagle's ascent stage lifted off from the Moon on 21 July, rejoined Columbia, and the reunited spacecraft returned to Earth. The command module splashed down in the North Pacific on 24 July 1969 at 16:50 UTC and was recovered by the USS Hornet. The three astronauts spent three weeks in quarantine, then were greeted with parades on three continents.

Apollo 11 was followed by five further successful lunar landings through 1972, delivering ten more Americans to the surface and returning several hundred kilograms of samples that reshaped planetary science. But public and political appetite for the programme faded quickly. No human being has returned to the Moon since the crew of Apollo 17 left it in December 1972.

Beyond the geopolitical victory, the mission left cultural artefacts that endure: the photograph of Aldrin standing on the plain with Armstrong reflected in his visor; the earthrise-and-flag television footage; the plaque left behind on the descent stage, reading 'We came in peace for all mankind.' It remains the clearest single moment in which a national project became, however briefly, a human one.

worldContext.text: China was in the later phase of the Cultural Revolution. The Ninth Congress of the Communist Party of China, held in April 1969, formally confirmed Lin Biao as Mao Zedong's designated successor. In March a series of armed clashes with Soviet forces on the Ussuri River at Zhenbao Island brought the two communist giants to the brink of open war.

The Vietnam War dominated Southeast Asia. President Nixon had begun the first American troop withdrawals from South Vietnam on 8 July 1969, announcing that 25,000 soldiers would be gone by September, while secretly extending the bombing campaign into Cambodia. Preliminary peace talks in Paris continued without result.

The Nigerian Civil War was in its third year. The breakaway Republic of Biafra, blockaded by federal forces, was in the grip of a catastrophic famine that had drawn international attention and airlifts of aid; the war would end with Biafra's collapse in January 1970.

In August 1969 British troops were deployed on the streets of Belfast and Londonderry after sectarian rioting, beginning Operation Banner and the long conflict known as the Troubles. A year after Warsaw Pact tanks had ended the Prague Spring, Czechoslovakia was under a hardening 'normalisation' regime led by Gustav Husak.

The War of Attrition between Israel and Egypt along the Suez Canal continued through 1969 in the aftermath of the 1967 Six-Day War. On 1 September a young colonel named Muammar Gaddafi led a bloodless coup in Libya, deposing King Idris and setting the country on a new revolutionary path.

figures.name: Neil Armstrong
figures.role: Mission commander; first human to walk on the Moon

figures.name: Buzz Aldrin
figures.role: Lunar module pilot; second human to walk on the Moon

figures.name: Michael Collins
figures.role: Command module pilot; remained in lunar orbit aboard Columbia while Armstrong and Aldrin descended

images.caption: Buzz Aldrin on the lunar surface near the leg of the Eagle lunar module, photographed by Neil Armstrong on 20 July 1969.
images.credit: Neil Armstrong / NASA, via Wikimedia Commons
